cyprus.name: Cyprus
cyprus.british_deaths_source: FCO consular data. Large British expat community concentrated in Paphos and Limassol. Significant tourist volume including elderly visitors.
cyprus.split_island_note: Cyprus is a divided island. The Republic of Cyprus (south) is internationally recognised. Northern Cyprus (Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus, TRNC) is recognised only by Turkey. British nationals dying in Northern Cyprus face additional complications as UK consular services operate differently there.

step_1_immediate.title: Immediate steps after death
step_1_immediate.details: Call 112 or 199 for ambulance, 199 for police. A doctor must certify the death. If death occurs outside hospital, police will attend. Contact the British High Commission in Nicosia. For deaths in the British Sovereign Base Areas (Akrotiri and Dhekelia), British military authorities may be involved.
step_1_immediate.who_to_contact_first: Emergency services (112/199), then British High Commission Nicosia
step_1_immediate.sovereign_bases_note: Akrotiri and Dhekelia are British Sovereign Base Areas. Deaths in these areas may involve British military authorities alongside Cypriot civil processes.
step_1_immediate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance on death in Cyprus

step_2_death_certificate.title: Death registration and certificate
step_2_death_certificate.details: Death registered at the local district administration office. A medical certificate from the doctor and a police report (if applicable) are required. Death certificate issued in Greek and English (bilingual). Cypriot death certificates show cause of death.
step_2_death_certificate.cost_est: Nominal fee
step_2_death_certificate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance on death in Cyprus

step_3_embassy_notification.title: British High Commission notification
step_3_embassy_notification.details: British High Commission in Nicosia must be notified. Cyprus also has British consular services in Paphos and Limassol, where the majority of British expat deaths occur.
embassy_can_do: Provide list of local funeral directors
Help contact family in the UK
Provide guidance on Cypriot procedures
Assist with Sovereign Base Area complications if applicable

step_4_embalming.title: Embalming and preparation
step_4_embalming.details: Embalming is required for international repatriation. Cypriot funeral directors handle embalming. Facilities are professional, particularly in Paphos and Limassol where British repatriations are frequent. Cyprus is a small island so logistics are straightforward.
step_4_embalming.notes: Cypriot funeral industry is experienced with British repatriations due to the large expat community. Quality is good.

step_5_coffin.title: Coffin requirements
step_5_coffin.details: Zinc-lined hermetically sealed coffin required for international air transport per IATA regulations. Available in major Cypriot cities.
step_5_coffin.notes: IATA requirement. Available across the Republic of Cyprus.

step_6_documentation.title: Documentation for repatriation
step_6_documentation.notes: Cypriot documentation is relatively straightforward. Bilingual certificates reduce translation needs. Small island size means fewer logistical complications. Funeral directors in Paphos and Limassol handle British repatriations routinely.

step_7_transport.title: Air transport to UK
step_7_transport.details: Body transported as human remains cargo on commercial flights. Larnaca Airport is the main international airport. Paphos Airport also has direct UK flights. Good flight connectivity to the UK.
step_7_transport.notes: Good flight frequency between Cyprus and the UK, particularly from Paphos and Larnaca. Multiple airlines operate seasonal and year-round services. Small island size means local transport to the airport is simple.

step_8_uk_reception.title: Reception in UK
step_8_uk_reception.details: UK funeral director receives body at the airport, completes customs clearance, and transports to funeral home.
step_8_uk_reception.customs_process: Straightforward documentation. Bilingual death certificate simplifies customs.
step_8_uk_reception.coroner_involvement: UK coroner may be involved if circumstances of death are unclear.
step_8_uk_reception.source: HMRC procedures; Coroners and Justice Act 2009

uae.name: United Arab Emirates
uae.british_deaths_source: FCO consular data. Large British expat community in Dubai and Abu Dhabi. Significant business and tourist travel.
uae.reverse_repatriation_note: The UAE is a major REVERSE repatriation origin. Large South Asian worker populations (Indian, Pakistani, Bangladeshi, Filipino) die in the UAE and require repatriation to their home countries. This reverse market is significant for content targeting.

step_1_immediate.title: Immediate steps after death
step_1_immediate.details: Call 999 for police or 998 for ambulance. In Dubai, call Dubai Police (901). A doctor must certify the death. All deaths must be reported to the police, who issue a police report. This police report is essential for all subsequent steps. Contact the British Embassy in Abu Dhabi or the British Consulate in Dubai.
step_1_immediate.who_to_contact_first: Police (999), then British Embassy Abu Dhabi or Consulate Dubai
step_1_immediate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance on death in the UAE

step_2_death_certificate.title: Death registration and certificate
step_2_death_certificate.details: The hospital issues a notification of death. This must be taken to the local health authority (DHA in Dubai, HAAD in Abu Dhabi) for the official death certificate. A police report is required before the death certificate is issued. Death certificates are issued in Arabic. An English translation may be available from the health authority or must be arranged separately. The death certificate requires attestation by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MOFA) for international use.
step_2_death_certificate.cost_est: AED 50-200 for certificate. MOFA attestation additional.
step_2_death_certificate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance; UAE health authority procedures

step_3_embassy_notification.title: Embassy/Consulate notification
step_3_embassy_notification.details: British Embassy in Abu Dhabi or British Consulate in Dubai must be notified. The Dubai consulate handles the majority of British death cases.
embassy_can_do: Provide list of local funeral directors
Help contact family in the UK
Provide guidance on UAE procedures
Liaise with UAE authorities
embassy_cannot_do: Intervene in UAE legal or Sharia processes

step_4_embalming.title: Embalming and preparation
step_4_embalming.details: Embalming is required for international repatriation. UAE has modern, professional embalming facilities, particularly in Dubai and Abu Dhabi. The UAE handles a very high volume of repatriations (South Asian workers) so the system is well-practised. Islamic law does not traditionally permit embalming, but UAE authorities accommodate it for international repatriation of non-Muslim deceased.
step_4_embalming.notes: UAE facilities are modern and professional. Dubai and Abu Dhabi handle thousands of repatriations annually (primarily South Asian workers). The system is efficient for international transport.

step_5_coffin.title: Coffin requirements
step_5_coffin.details: Zinc-lined hermetically sealed coffin required for international air transport per IATA regulations. Readily available in Dubai and Abu Dhabi.
step_5_coffin.notes: IATA requirement. UAE funeral services suppliers are well-stocked due to high repatriation volume.

step_6_documentation.title: Documentation for repatriation
step_6_documentation.notes: UAE bureaucracy is efficient by regional standards but requires multiple steps. The police clearance/NOC is essential and can be delayed if any investigation is pending. Islamic weekend (Friday-Saturday in some emirates, though many shifted to Saturday-Sunday) affects office hours. MOFA attestation is a required step for international validity.
step_6_documentation.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance; UAE government procedures

step_7_transport.title: Air transport to UK
step_7_transport.details: Body transported as human remains cargo on commercial flights. Excellent aviation infrastructure. Emirates and Etihad have among the world's best cargo services. Multiple daily flights to London and other UK airports. Dubai and Abu Dhabi are major cargo hubs.
step_7_transport.notes: The UAE has some of the best cargo infrastructure in the world. Emirates SkyCargo and Etihad Cargo handle huge volumes of human remains annually (primarily South Asian workers being repatriated). The system is efficient and well-practised. Multiple daily flights to multiple UK airports.

step_8_uk_reception.title: Reception in UK
step_8_uk_reception.details: UK funeral director receives body at the airport, completes customs clearance, and transports to funeral home.
step_8_uk_reception.customs_process: HMRC documentation required. Death certificate and police clearance documentation needed.
step_8_uk_reception.coroner_involvement: UK coroner may be involved depending on circumstances.

cultural_practices.notes: The UAE's massive expatriate population (over 80% of residents are non-Emirati) means the system is well-practised at handling deaths of foreign nationals from many countries and faiths. This makes the process more streamlined than many other Middle Eastern destinations.
cultural_practices.source: General knowledge of UAE customs; FCDO guidance

practical_information.language: Arabic (official). English is widely used as the de facto business and daily language.
practical_information.english_widely_spoken: Very widely spoken. English is the working language of most businesses, hospitals, and government services that deal with expatriates. Documentation available in both Arabic and English in many cases.
practical_information.healthcare_system: Excellent modern healthcare facilities, particularly in Dubai and Abu Dhabi. World-class private hospitals. Healthcare costs are covered by mandatory health insurance for residents. Tourist healthcare costs can be high.
practical_information.british_expat_population: ~120,000-240,000 British residents in the UAE (primarily Dubai and Abu Dhabi)
common_death_causes_abroad: Cardiac events (heat stress, particularly in summer)
Construction and industrial accidents (more relevant for South Asian workers)
high_risk_areas: Dubai: Largest British population. Road accidents, natural causes, construction.
Abu Dhabi: Second largest British community.
Northern emirates (Sharjah, Ajman, RAK): Less developed infrastructure.
Desert areas: Heat exposure, off-road accidents.
practical_information.time_zone: GST (UTC+4). 4 hours ahead of UK. 3 hours during BST.

ashes_transport.cremation_facilities: Hindu cremation facilities available in Dubai and Abu Dhabi. Not available for Muslim deceased under UAE law.
ashes_transport.can_carry_on_plane: Generally yes, with documentation. Emirates and Etihad accommodate ashes transport.
ashes_transport.critical_warning: Do NOT cremate abroad if a UK coroner may need to hold an inquest. Cremation destroys evidence.

philippines.name: Philippines
philippines.british_deaths_source: FCO consular data. Mix of elderly retirees, tourists, and those visiting Filipino spouses/partners. Also a significant reverse repatriation origin: large Filipino community in the UK.
philippines.reverse_repatriation_note: The Philippines has a large diaspora in the UK (estimated 150,000-200,000 Filipino nationals). Reverse repatriation of Filipino workers from the UK to the Philippines is a significant market.

step_1_immediate.title: Immediate steps after death
step_1_immediate.details: Contact local emergency services (911 in Metro Manila, or local police). If death occurs in hospital, the hospital handles initial procedures. If death is unexpected, police must be notified. In provincial areas, the local barangay captain may be involved. Contact the British Embassy in Manila.
step_1_immediate.who_to_contact_first: Local police or hospital, then British Embassy Manila
step_1_immediate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance on death in the Philippines

step_2_death_certificate.title: Death registration and certificate
step_2_death_certificate.details: Death must be registered with the local civil registrar. The certificate of death is issued by the attending physician and filed with the Local Civil Registry. A copy must be authenticated by the Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) for international use. The death certificate also requires Department of Foreign Affairs (DFA) authentication for international use. Filipino death certificates show cause of death and are issued in English.
step_2_death_certificate.processing_time: 5-14 days (PSA and DFA authentication can take weeks)
step_2_death_certificate.cost_est: PHP 500-2,000 for certificates and authentication
step_2_death_certificate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance; Philippine civil registration

step_3_embassy_notification.title: Embassy notification
step_3_embassy_notification.details: British Embassy in Manila must be notified. There is limited British consular presence outside Metro Manila.
embassy_can_do: Provide list of local funeral directors
Help contact family in the UK
Provide guidance on Philippine procedures
Liaise with Philippine authorities
step_3_embassy_notification.no_provincial_consulate: There is no British consulate outside Metro Manila. All consular services for provincial, island, and resort deaths are handled from Manila.

step_4_embalming.title: Embalming and preparation
step_4_embalming.details: Embalming is standard practice in the Philippines (Filipino funeral tradition involves extended wakes with open casket). Professional embalming is widely available in Metro Manila and major cities. Quality is generally good in urban areas. On islands and in provincial areas, embalming quality may be variable. The tropical climate makes prompt embalming essential.
step_4_embalming.urgency_note: URGENT. Tropical climate requires immediate embalming or refrigeration.
step_4_embalming.quality_note: Metro Manila and Cebu have professional facilities. Provincial and island embalming may be less reliable. Body may need to be transported to Manila for quality embalming.

step_5_coffin.title: Coffin requirements
step_5_coffin.details: Zinc-lined hermetically sealed coffin required for international air transport per IATA regulations. Available in Metro Manila and Cebu.
step_5_coffin.notes: IATA-spec coffins available in Manila and Cebu. Provincial areas may need coffins sourced from Manila.

step_6_documentation.title: Documentation for repatriation
documents_needed: Freedom from infection / quarantine clearance from Bureau of Quarantine
step_6_documentation.processing_time: 3-6 weeks (PSA and DFA authentication are the main delays)
step_6_documentation.notes: Philippine documentation is complex and slow. The dual PSA and DFA authentication requirements add weeks. Bureau of Quarantine clearance is an additional step. Provincial deaths require documents to be processed in Manila, adding internal logistics. The 3-6 week timeline is the norm, not the exception.
step_6_documentation.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance; Philippine government procedures

step_7_transport.title: Air transport to UK
step_7_transport.details: Body transported as human remains cargo on commercial flights. Manila (NAIA) is the main cargo hub. Philippine Airlines and connecting carriers handle cargo. No direct flights to the UK: connections via a Gulf hub (Dubai, Doha) or Hong Kong/Singapore are standard. Island and provincial deaths require internal transport to Manila first.
step_7_transport.internal_transport_note: Deaths outside Metro Manila (Cebu, Boracay, Palawan, Davao, provincial areas) require internal transport to Manila. Island geography makes this particularly complex: ferry and domestic flights are the options. Weather disruptions (typhoon season June-November) can affect internal transport.
step_7_transport.notes: No direct Philippines-UK flights. All routes require at least one connection. Long journey times and double cargo handling add to costs. Typhoon season (June-November) can disrupt both internal and international flights.

step_8_uk_reception.title: Reception in UK
step_8_uk_reception.details: UK funeral director receives body at Heathrow (primary arrival point). Customs clearance and transfer to funeral home.
step_8_uk_reception.customs_process: HMRC documentation required.
step_8_uk_reception.coroner_involvement: UK coroner may be involved depending on circumstances.
step_8_uk_reception.source: HMRC procedures; Coroners and Justice Act 2009

The extended timeline (3-6 weeks) increases storage and agent fees. Provincial deaths are more expensive than Manila deaths due to internal logistics.
cost_guide.insurance_note: Travel insurance with repatriation cover is essential. Without insurance, the 3-6 week process and GBP 5,000-12,000 cost is a significant burden.

embassy_contacts.no_provincial_presence: No British consular presence outside Metro Manila. All cases handled from Manila.

cultural_practices.dominant_religion: Roman Catholic (~80%). Islam in Mindanao and Sulu (~5-10%). Protestant and other Christian denominations.
cultural_practices.burial_vs_cremation: Burial is the predominant practice. Cremation is growing in urban areas but remains a minority choice. Catholic tradition favours burial.
cultural_practices.funeral_timing: Filipino funerals traditionally involve an extended wake (lamay) lasting 3-7 days or longer before burial. This is one of the longest wake traditions in the world. Open casket is standard.
cultural_practices.body_handling: Embalming is standard Filipino practice due to the extended wake tradition. Professional embalming widely available in urban areas.
cultural_practices.religious_considerations: Catholic funeral mass is the standard. Protestant, Islamic (in Mindanao), and other religious ceremonies available.
local_customs: Lamay (wake/vigil) lasting 3-7 days or more is the central tradition. Family and community gather continuously.
Open casket is standard, with professional embalming and dressing of the deceased
Pagdasal (prayers) held nightly during the wake
Libing (burial) follows the extended wake
Black or white clothing worn for mourning
Pasiyam: nine-day prayer novena after burial
Food is served throughout the wake period to visitors
Superstitions (pasma) about handling the deceased are taken seriously in some communities
cultural_practices.notes: Filipino funeral culture is among the most elaborate in the world. The extended wake tradition means embalming is culturally normalised, which benefits the repatriation process. British families should be aware that the Filipino community may expect extended mourning rituals.
cultural_practices.source: General knowledge of Filipino funeral customs

practical_information.language: Filipino (Tagalog) and English (both official). English widely spoken.
practical_information.english_widely_spoken: Very widely spoken. The Philippines is one of the largest English-speaking countries in the world. Official documents issued in English. Hospital and police staff generally speak English, though provincial areas may be less fluent.
practical_information.healthcare_system: Good private hospitals in Metro Manila (St. Luke's, Makati Medical Center, The Medical City). Provincial and island healthcare varies from adequate to very basic. Rural health facilities are limited. Island healthcare may be rudimentary.
common_death_causes_abroad: Road traffic accidents (chaotic traffic, motorcycle accidents)
high_risk_areas: Metro Manila: Traffic accidents, natural causes
Cebu: Tourist volume, diving, drowning
Boracay: Tourist volume, drowning
Palawan: Adventure tourism, diving, remote location

practical_information.time_zone: PHT (UTC+8). 8 hours ahead of UK. 7 hours during BST.

ashes_transport.cremation_facilities: Available in Metro Manila and major cities (Cebu, Davao). Limited in provincial areas and on smaller islands.
ashes_transport.can_carry_on_plane: Generally yes, with documentation. Notify airline in advance.
ashes_transport.typical_cost_ashes_return_gbp: GBP 500-1,500 (flight ticket to carry personally, long-haul with connection)
ashes_transport.cost_saving_note: Cremation in the Philippines and carrying ashes home (GBP 1,000-3,000 total) is significantly cheaper than full body repatriation (GBP 5,000-12,000). The simplified documentation also reduces the timeline substantially.
ashes_transport.critical_warning: Do NOT cremate abroad if a UK coroner may need to hold an inquest.
ashes_transport.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance; Philippine cremation regulations

south-africa.name: South Africa
south-africa.british_deaths_source: FCO consular data. Tourist deaths (safari, Cape Town, Durban) and elderly British expat community. Crime-related deaths are a notable proportion.

step_1_immediate.title: Immediate steps after death
step_1_immediate.details: Call 10111 for police or 10177 for ambulance. If death occurs in hospital, the hospital handles initial procedures. For deaths outside hospital, South African Police Service (SAPS) must attend. Crime-related deaths trigger SAPS investigation. Contact the British High Commission in Pretoria or nearest consulate.
step_1_immediate.who_to_contact_first: Police (10111) or ambulance (10177), then British High Commission or nearest consulate
step_1_immediate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance on death in South Africa

step_2_death_certificate.title: Death registration and certificate
step_2_death_certificate.details: Death must be registered with the Department of Home Affairs within 72 hours. The doctor or hospital issues the notification of death (DHA-1663 form). The Department of Home Affairs issues the official death certificate. Issued in English. For unnatural deaths, the death certificate is only issued after the inquest is complete, which can take months.
step_2_death_certificate.processing_time: 3-14 days for natural deaths. Months for unnatural deaths (awaiting inquest).
step_2_death_certificate.cost_est: Free
step_2_death_certificate.unnatural_death_delay: IMPORTANT: For unnatural deaths (including crime-related), the death certificate is only issued after the medico-legal inquest is complete. This can take months and significantly delays repatriation.
step_2_death_certificate.source: South African Births and Deaths Registration Act; gov.uk FCDO guidance

step_3_embassy_notification.title: British High Commission notification
step_3_embassy_notification.details: British High Commission in Pretoria or the nearest consulate must be notified. South Africa has good British consular coverage in major cities.
embassy_can_do: Provide list of local funeral directors
Help contact family in the UK
Provide guidance on South African procedures
Liaise with SAPS on family's behalf
embassy_cannot_do: Intervene in South African legal or police processes

step_4_embalming.title: Embalming and preparation
step_4_embalming.details: Embalming is required for international repatriation. South African funeral industry is professional and well-developed, with standards comparable to European countries. Embalming quality is consistently good in major cities (Johannesburg, Cape Town, Durban).
step_4_embalming.notes: South African funeral industry is professional and experienced. Good embalming standards across major cities. Rural areas may have more basic facilities.

step_5_coffin.title: Coffin requirements
step_5_coffin.details: Zinc-lined hermetically sealed coffin required for international air transport per IATA regulations. Available in all major South African cities.
step_5_coffin.notes: IATA requirement. Available from professional funeral directors in major cities.

step_6_documentation.title: Documentation for repatriation
step_6_documentation.processing_time: 7-14 days for natural deaths. Can be months for unnatural deaths.
step_6_documentation.notes: Documentation is relatively efficient for natural deaths in major cities. Unnatural deaths (crime, accidents) trigger medico-legal investigations that can delay the death certificate by months. The South African Police Service must provide clearance for all repatriations.
step_6_documentation.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance; South African regulations

step_7_transport.title: Air transport to UK
step_7_transport.details: Body transported as human remains cargo on commercial flights. Johannesburg O.R. Tambo International Airport is the main cargo hub. Cape Town and Durban also have international cargo capacity. South African Airways (if operating) and British Airways provide cargo services.
step_7_transport.notes: Good direct flight connectivity from Johannesburg and Cape Town to London Heathrow. Durban deaths may require internal transport to Johannesburg. Safari deaths in national parks (Kruger, etc.) require road transport to the nearest city with a cargo airport.

step_8_uk_reception.title: Reception in UK
step_8_uk_reception.details: UK funeral director receives body at Heathrow (primary). Customs clearance and transfer to funeral home.
step_8_uk_reception.customs_process: HMRC documentation required.
step_8_uk_reception.coroner_involvement: UK coroner may be involved, particularly for crime-related or suspicious deaths.
step_8_uk_reception.source: HMRC procedures; Coroners and Justice Act 2009

cultural_practices.funeral_timing: Funerals typically held on Saturdays in Black South African tradition. 1-2 weeks after death to allow family to gather. White South African and mixed communities often follow 3-7 day timing.
cultural_practices.body_handling: Professional handling. South African funeral industry is well-developed. Embalming is standard practice.
cultural_practices.religious_considerations: Diverse. Christian (multiple denominations), traditional African, Muslim, Hindu, Jewish ceremonies all available.

kenya.name: Kenya
kenya.british_deaths_source: FCO consular data. Mix of safari tourists, coastal resort visitors (Mombasa, Diani), and British expats/development workers in Nairobi.

step_1_immediate.title: Immediate steps after death
step_1_immediate.details: Contact emergency services (999 or 112). If death occurs outside hospital, police must attend. The police arrange removal to a mortuary. Contact the British High Commission in Nairobi.

step_1_immediate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance on death in Kenya

step_2_death_certificate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance; Kenyan Births and Deaths Registration Act

step_3_embassy_notification.title: British High Commission notification

step_4_embalming.title: Embalming and preparation
step_4_embalming.details: Embalming is required for international repatriation. Nairobi has professional embalming facilities. Quality in Nairobi is generally good. Mombasa and coastal areas have adequate facilities. Safari locations and rural areas may have very limited or no embalming capability, requiring body transport to Nairobi.
step_4_embalming.quality_note: Nairobi embalming quality is adequate for repatriation. Outside Nairobi, quality is variable. Safari deaths typically require transport to Nairobi for proper embalming.

step_5_coffin.title: Coffin requirements
step_5_coffin.details: Zinc-lined hermetically sealed coffin required for international air transport per IATA regulations. Available in Nairobi. May need to be sourced from Nairobi for deaths elsewhere.

step_6_documentation.title: Documentation for repatriation
documents_needed: Freedom from infection certificate from Port Health
step_6_documentation.notes: Kenyan documentation can be slow, particularly for the death certificate and police clearance. Nairobi processes are faster than provincial offices. Port Health clearance (freedom from infection) is an additional step. Safari deaths in remote areas add significant internal transport time before documentation can even begin.

step_7_transport.title: Air transport to UK
step_7_transport.details: Body transported as human remains cargo on commercial flights. Nairobi JKIA is the main cargo hub. Kenya Airways has good cargo services. British Airways also operates direct London-Nairobi. Mombasa has limited international cargo capacity; most repatriations route through Nairobi.
step_7_transport.internal_transport_note: Safari deaths (Masai Mara, Amboseli, Tsavo, Samburu) require road transport to Nairobi. This can take 4-8 hours depending on location and road conditions. Mombasa coast deaths may require road or domestic flight to Nairobi.
step_7_transport.notes: Good direct Nairobi-London connectivity (KQ and BA). Safari deaths are the main internal logistics challenge. Masai Mara is 5-6 hours by road from Nairobi. Light aircraft charters from safari airstrips can speed up internal transport but add cost.

canada.name: Canada
canada.british_deaths_source: FCO consular data. Combination of tourist deaths, elderly visitors, working holiday visa holders, and British expat community.

step_1_immediate.title: Immediate steps after death
step_1_immediate.details: Call 911 for emergency services. If death occurs in hospital, the hospital handles initial procedures. Unexpected deaths may trigger coroner or medical examiner involvement (system varies by province). Contact the British High Commission in Ottawa or nearest consulate.
step_1_immediate.who_to_contact_first: Emergency services (911), then British High Commission or nearest consulate
step_1_immediate.provincial_variation_note: Canada has 10 provinces and 3 territories, each with its own death investigation system (some use coroners, others use medical examiners), vital statistics registration, and funeral regulations.
step_1_immediate.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance on death in Canada

step_2_death_certificate.title: Death registration and certificate
step_2_death_certificate.details: Death must be registered with the provincial or territorial Vital Statistics office. The funeral director normally handles registration. Death certificates are issued in English (and French in Quebec and bilingual jurisdictions). Canadian death certificates show cause of death. Multiple certified copies available.
step_2_death_certificate.cost_est: CAD 25-75 per certified copy (varies by province)
documents_needed: Medical certificate of death from doctor
step_2_death_certificate.source: Provincial Vital Statistics offices; gov.uk FCDO guidance

step_3_embassy_notification.title: British High Commission notification
step_3_embassy_notification.details: British High Commission in Ottawa or nearest consulate must be notified. Canada has good British consular coverage across major cities.
embassy_can_do: Provide list of local funeral directors
Help contact family in the UK
Provide guidance on Canadian provincial procedures
Issue consular death certificate if needed

step_4_embalming.title: Embalming and preparation
step_4_embalming.details: Embalming is required for international repatriation. Canadian funeral industry is professional and well-regulated, with standards comparable to the UK and USA. Embalming quality is consistently high across the country.
step_4_embalming.notes: Canadian funeral industry operates to very high standards. No quality concerns.

step_5_coffin.title: Coffin requirements
step_5_coffin.details: Zinc-lined hermetically sealed coffin or air tray required for international air transport per IATA regulations. Readily available across Canada.
step_5_coffin.notes: IATA requirement. Widely available. Canadian funeral directors experienced with international transport.

step_6_documentation.title: Documentation for repatriation
step_6_documentation.notes: Canadian documentation is efficient and English-language (French in Quebec, but bilingual options available). Provincial variation exists but all systems are well-organised. No translation required for English provinces.

step_7_transport.title: Air transport to UK
step_7_transport.details: Body transported as human remains cargo on commercial flights. Toronto Pearson YYZ is the main Eastern Canada cargo hub. Vancouver YVR is the main Western Canada hub. Air Canada has excellent cargo services. British Airways also operates direct routes.
step_7_transport.internal_transport_note: Deaths in remote areas, mountain resorts (Banff, Whistler), northern provinces/territories, or Atlantic provinces may require internal transport to Toronto or Vancouver. Canada's vast size (second largest country in the world) means internal distances can be enormous.
step_7_transport.notes: Good direct Toronto and Vancouver to London connectivity. Eastern Canadian deaths are faster and cheaper to repatriate due to shorter flight. Western/northern Canadian deaths add internal transport costs. Remote northern deaths (Yukon, Northwest Territories, Nunavut) can involve extremely complex logistics.

step_8_uk_reception.title: Reception in UK
step_8_uk_reception.details: UK funeral director receives body at Heathrow (primary). Customs clearance and transfer to funeral home.
step_8_uk_reception.customs_process: HMRC documentation required. English-language paperwork simplifies customs.
step_8_uk_reception.coroner_involvement: UK coroner may be involved depending on circumstances.
step_8_uk_reception.source: HMRC procedures; Coroners and Justice Act 2009

Remote location (northern territories, mountain resorts, wilderness areas)

cost_guide.cost_notes: Canada is moderately expensive. Canadian funeral industry costs are comparable to the USA (higher than European equivalents). Toronto and Vancouver are the most expensive. Eastern Canada repatriations are slightly cheaper due to shorter flight distance. Remote/northern deaths add substantial internal transport. Ski resort deaths (Whistler, Banff) add moderate internal transport.
cost_guide.insurance_note: Travel insurance with repatriation cover is essential. Canadian healthcare costs (while lower than the USA) can be significant for uninsured visitors.

cultural_practices.burial_vs_cremation: Cremation is the majority choice (approximately 73% nationally, one of the highest rates in the world). Burial remains common among Catholic, Orthodox, Muslim, and some immigrant communities.
cultural_practices.funeral_timing: Funerals typically 3-7 days after death, similar to UK and US norms.
cultural_practices.body_handling: Professional handling. Canadian funeral industry standards comparable to UK and USA.
cultural_practices.religious_considerations: Highly diverse. Catholic, Protestant, secular, Sikh, Hindu, Muslim, Buddhist, Jewish, Indigenous First Nations ceremonies all available.

cultural_practices.source: General knowledge of Canadian funeral customs

practical_information.language: English and French (both official). English dominant in all provinces except Quebec.
practical_information.english_widely_spoken: Yes. No language barrier except in parts of Quebec. English documentation available in all provinces.
practical_information.healthcare_system: Excellent public healthcare (Medicare). Non-residents are not covered by provincial health insurance. Private travel insurance needed. Hospital standards comparable to UK.

police_contact.rcmp: Contact via 911 in areas served by RCMP

common_death_causes_abroad: Ski and snowboard accidents (Whistler, Banff, Tremblant)
Bear and wildlife encounters (rare but notable)
high_risk_areas: Toronto and Ontario: Largest British community, tourist volume
Vancouver and BC: Tourist volume, ski resorts, wilderness
Calgary and Alberta: Ski resorts (Banff), road accidents
Quebec: Ski resorts (Tremblant), winter sports
Northern territories: Extreme remote, limited medical access
practical_information.time_zone: Multiple zones: EST/EDT (UTC-5/-4), CST/CDT (UTC-6/-5), MST/MDT (UTC-7/-6), PST/PDT (UTC-8/-7), plus Atlantic and Newfoundland. 5-8 hours behind UK.

ashes_transport.cremation_facilities: Widely available across Canada. One of the highest national cremation rates in the world (~73%).
ashes_transport.can_carry_on_plane: Yes, with documentation. Air Canada and other carriers allow ashes in carry-on. CATSA (Canadian Air Transport Security Authority) allows ashes through screening.
ashes_transport.cost_saving_note: Cremation in Canada and carrying ashes home saves significantly versus full body repatriation.
ashes_transport.critical_warning: Do NOT cremate if a UK coroner may need to hold an inquest.
ashes_transport.source: gov.uk FCDO guidance; CATSA regulations; airline policies

content_angles.primary_angle: Canada combines familiar English-speaking systems with the complication of vast geography and provincial regulatory variation. The process itself is straightforward (similar to the USA but with fewer hospital bill complications). The main challenges are internal transport for remote/wilderness/ski resort deaths and the distance-driven freight costs. Provincial variation (10 provinces, 3 territories, each with their own systems) mirrors the USA's state-by-state complexity.

Good British consular coverage across major cities

cultural_practices.dominant_religion: Muslim (~87% nationally). Bali: Hindu (~83% of island population).
cultural_practices.burial_vs_cremation: Muslim Indonesia: burial. Hindu Bali: cremation (ngaben ceremony, elaborate ritual).
cultural_practices.funeral_timing: Muslim: burial within 24 hours. Hindu Bali: cremation can be delayed for auspicious date (months sometimes).
local_customs: Bali: Ngaben (cremation ceremony) is elaborate and sacred. Hindu Balinese may offer to perform ngaben for deceased visitors.
Muslim Java/Sumatra: Islamic burial customs, washing, shrouding, quick burial
Banjar (village council) in Bali has authority over local ceremonies
cultural_practices.notes: The cultural gap between Hindu Bali and Muslim Java is vast. Content must differentiate.

practical_information.english_widely_spoken: Bali tourist areas yes. Java limited outside Jakarta.
high_risk_areas: Bali beaches (rip currents)
Kuta/Seminyak nightlife (alcohol)
Nusa Penida cliffs
Gili Islands (limited medical)

ashes_transport.notes: Cremation available in Bali (culturally standard) and Jakarta. Hindu ngaben ceremony is an option for families who wish it.

content_angles.primary_angle: Bali dominates (~90% of British deaths in Indonesia). The Hindu-Bali vs Muslim-Java cultural split is unique. Volcanic disruption is a real logistical threat. Inter-island transport for deaths outside Bali is complex.

step_1_immediate.title: Immediate steps after death
step_1_immediate.details: Contact local police (117) or ambulance (119). Hospital capacity varies enormously. Contact British Embassy Phnom Penh.
step_1_immediate.who_to_contact_first: Local police or hospital, then British Embassy Phnom Penh

step_2_death_certificate.title: Death registration
step_2_death_certificate.details: Death certificate from commune council (Sangkat). Police report required. Process can be slow and requires patience with bureaucracy.

step_3_embassy_notification.title: Embassy notification
step_3_embassy_notification.details: British Embassy Phnom Penh. Limited consular capacity for remote areas (Siem Reap, Sihanoukville).

step_4_embalming.title: Embalming
step_4_embalming.details: Limited embalming facilities. Best quality in Phnom Penh. Bodies from Siem Reap or Sihanoukville may need to be transported to Phnom Penh first.
step_4_embalming.notes: Quality is basic. Transfer to Phnom Penh recommended for proper embalming.

step_6_documentation.title: Documentation
step_6_documentation.notes: Cambodian bureaucracy is slow. Embassy assistance recommended throughout.

step_7_transport.title: Air transport
step_7_transport.details: Very limited direct cargo options. Usually routes via Bangkok (Thai Airways) or Singapore (SQ). Cambodia Angkor Air has minimal cargo capacity.

step_8_uk_reception.title: UK reception
step_8_uk_reception.details: Standard reception at Heathrow.
